query: what is the definition of latent phase of labor according to ACOG?

retrieved_docs.title: Normal Labor: Physiology, Evaluation, and Management -- Pathophysiology -- First stage of labor
retrieved_docs.content: The American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) supports using Zhang's curve data and recommends, in their Clinical Practice Guideline #8, that a prolonged latent phase be defined as lasting more than 16 hr, regardless of parity. However, most individuals with a prolonged latent phase will either stop contracting or progress to the active phase with oxytocin or amniotomy. For this reason, cesarean birth is not appropriate for a prolonged latent phase in individuals with spontaneous contractions.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pregnancy Contractions -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Understanding the normal pattern of contractions is also useful in defining the arrest of labor, which has different management based on the stage. For example, an arrested latent phase is an indication of augmentation with oxytocin, while an arrested active phase is an indication of the cesarean section. Defining the true arrest of labor requires analysis of the adequacy of uterine contractions, which requires the use of an intrauterine pressure catheter. Active phase arrest has specific requirements, which include 4 or more hours of adequate contractions or 6 or more hours of inadequate contractions. These definitions are essential for clinicians because they dictate management algorithms.

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm Labor -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: PTL is diagnosed clinically based on symptoms and physical findings. ACOG's Practice Bulletin on the management of PTL states that the diagnosis of PTL is "generally based on clinical criteria of regular uterine contractions accompanied by a change in cervical dilation, effacement, or both, or initial presentation with regular contractions and cervical dilation of at least 2 cm." [1] The threshold for defining "regular contractions" has varied in the literature, ranging from 4 to 12 contractions per hour. [2] ACOG does not include a threshold in their definition, though the WAPM-PMF joint guideline recommends requiring 6 or more contractions per 30 minutes. [1] [2] WAPM-PMF recommends observing patients for at least 2 hours to assess contraction frequency and cervical change.

retrieved_docs.title: Early Decelerations -- Nursing, Allied Health, and Interprofessional Team Interventions
retrieved_docs.content: Current evidence does not demonstrate a definitive advantage of continuous fetal monitoring over intermittent auscultation. Therefore, either approach is acceptable for uncomplicated patients. The ACOG recommends continuous FHR monitoring for high-risk patients, such as those with known intrauterine growth restriction or preeclampsia. When using continuous FHR monitoring during labor, healthcare providers should periodically review and document the tracings. In uncomplicated patients with Category I FHR tracings, reviews should occur every 30 minutes during the first stage of labor and every 15 minutes during the second stage. Documentation should include a description of the FHR category and the overall pattern of the FHR. Management changes may occur in response to the development of Category II or Category III characteristics. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Normal Labor: Physiology, Evaluation, and Management -- Pathophysiology -- First stage of labor
retrieved_docs.content: The latent phase may be longer for parturients undergoing induction of labor. When maternal and fetal status remains reassuring, the ACOG recommends allowing 12 to 18 hr of oxytocin administration after rupture of membranes before diagnosing a failed induction of labor.

retrieved_docs.title: Induction of Labor -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Indications for late preterm, early term, late-term, and post-term delivery timing depend on a patient's obstetrical and medical history. IOL is indicated when it is thought that the outcomes for the fetus, the mother, or both are better than with expectant management, that is, waiting for the spontaneous onset of labor. [3] The American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) has an extensive list of recommendations on delivery timing, with some of the more common clinical scenarios listed below [4] .

retrieved_docs.title: Early Pregnancy Loss (Spontaneous Abortion) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The terminology used to describe the loss of an intrauterine pregnancy before fetal viability (ie, a fetus likely to survive outside of the uterus) is inconsistent in the literature and has varied over time. [1] [2] Traditionally, spontaneous abortion referred to a natural pregnancy loss before 20 weeks of gestation; however, more recently, the medical literature has been transitioning away from the phrase. [3] Patients prefer the term miscarriage , presumably to avoid the stigma associated with induced abortion. [1] The reVITALize gynecology definitions from the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG), which are also endorsed by the American Academy of Nurse-Midwives, the American Academy of Family Physicians, and numerous other American professional organizations, now recommend the terms miscarriage or intrauterine pregnancy loss . Both terms are synonymous and are defined as the loss of a documented nonviable (ie, a pregnancy not expected to develop normally) intrauterine pregnancy. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Initial Antepartum Care -- Function -- Pregnancy confirmation
retrieved_docs.content: Many women of reproductive age will present for evaluation of various complaints for which pregnancy is the differential diagnosis that should be excluded first, including amenorrhea, abdominal pain, and irregular menses. If pregnancy has not been confirmed by ultrasound imaging before the first visit, a urine or serum beta-human chorionic gonadotropin (β-hCG) should be performed. This hormone comprises dissimilar alpha and beta subunits required for biological activity. β-hCG levels between the serum and urine specimens correlate well but depend on urine-specific gravity. Ideally, a first void is preferred for urine specimens due to increased concentration. [16] However, an elevated β-hCG level is not definitive of a normal or viable pregnancy. Differential diagnoses that may result in elevated β-hCG levels must be considered, including ectopic and heterotopic pregnancy, miscarriage, and gestational trophoblastic disease. Confirmation of a viable intrauterine pregnancy requires ultrasound imaging. Typically, a normal intrauterine pregnancy is visible on transvaginal ultrasound at approximately 5 to 6 weeks gestation. However, in patients with an unknown or unreliable last menstrual period (LMP) or indications for an early ultrasound (eg, vaginal bleeding and pelvic pain), a quantitative serum β-hCG can be used to interpret ultrasound findings. The "discriminatory zone" refers to the quantitative β-hCG value at which a gestational sac should be seen within the endometrial cavity by transvaginal ultrasonography for an intrauterine pregnancy. The parameters for discriminatory zones vary depending on several factors; therefore, its use is debated, but the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) generally suggests using a high β-hCG threshold of approximately 3500 mIU/mL to prevent misdiagnosis of a viable intrauterine gestation. Furthermore, in patients with a serum quantitative β-hCG level under the discriminatory zone or clinical features of an abnormal pregnancy, a repeat β-hCG may be performed; an approximate doubling of β-hCG levels in 48 hours is reassuring of a viable pregnancy in the early first trimester. Usually, the β-hCG level will continue to increase in this fashion until 10 weeks gestation, plateauing at approximately 100,000 mIU/mL. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Patient Self-Determination Act -- Definition/Introduction

(c) Effective Dates- (1) The amendments made by subsection (a) shall apply with respect to services furnished on or after the first day of the first month beginning more than 180 days after the date of the enactment of this Act. (2) The amendment made by subsection (b) shall apply to contracts under section 1876 of the Social Security Act as of the first day of the first month beginning more than 180 days after the date of the enactment of this Act

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm Labor -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Preterm labor (PTL) is defined as cervical change that develops in response to regular uterine contractions occurring between 20 0/7 and 36 6/7 weeks of estimated gestational age (EGA). The American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) also states that PTL can be diagnosed in patients with regular contractions and cervical dilation of at least 2 cm before 37 weeks EGA, while the international guidelines from the World Association of Perinatal Medicine and the Perinatal Medicine Foundation (WAPM-PMF) recommend using a cutoff of at least 3 cm to diagnose spontaneous preterm labor (sPTL). [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Normal Labor: Physiology, Evaluation, and Management -- Pathophysiology -- Stages of Labor
retrieved_docs.content: The stages of labor have been clearly defined to establish universal terminology, enabling medical professionals to communicate effectively. These definitions have facilitated the development of guidelines that distinguish normal from abnormal labor patterns and allow professional organizations to recommend stage-specific interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: Symptothermal Contraception -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: To reliably predict the upcoming menstrual cycles, it has been suggested that data collection should be done for approximately 6 to 12 cycles. [9] Using all of this information, the woman may then chart her menstrual cycle and determine which days they are in the luteal phase, the follicular phase, and which day ovulation is most likely to occur. The woman may then be able to determine their "fertile window," which is the period of days around ovulation when conception is most likely to occur. [10] The ovum remains able to be fertilized for 12 to 24 hours after ovulation. Sperm can live for up to 5 days after deposition in the female genital tract. These 2 considerations result in a fertile window of approximately 6 days. The highest chance of achieving pregnancy occurs in the 2 days before ovulation. Since a person does not usually ovulate on the same day with every cycle, the fertile window is considered to be longer than the actual 6 days with the FABMs of contraception. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: HCQM Two-Midnight Rule -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The level of care provided to patients is not a one-size-fits-all approach but one that varies with the clinical presentation and the clinician's professional judgment regarding the expected treatment course. It is also essential for healthcare staff to understand that the "two-midnight rule" primarily applies to Medicare beneficiaries; private payers have their own screening criteria to determine the appropriate level of care.

retrieved_docs.title: Episiotomy -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The long-term effects studied in follow-up research regarding episiotomy revealed no difference in urinary issues, anal incontinence, or painful intercourse (ie, dyspareunia) between the spontaneous lacerations and episiotomy groups. Despite this, evidence remains limited, and larger trials are needed to clarify the role of episiotomy in complex deliveries. [7] Subsequently, the  American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) has stated that no definite indications for episiotomy have been established. Consequently, the current recommendation is to utilize restrictive episiotomy, in which episiotomy is considered based on clinical factors, including nonreassuring fetal heart tones and assisted vaginal births with vacuum or forceps, particularly in nulliparous patients. In these cases, studies have shown that mediolateral episiotomy significantly reduces the risk of obstetrical anal sphincter injuries. [9] [11]

retrieved_docs.content: When determining medical necessity, utilization management professionals carefully evaluate the severity of an illness or condition and analyze the intensity of the required medical services. This thorough assessment allows the healthcare team to make informed decisions regarding the level of care most suitable for the needs of the patient.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2843
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical induction of labor is classified as a specific type of induction and delivery procedure, meaning it falls under the broader category of interventions used to initiate and manage childbirth. Recognizing surgical induction of labor as a subset of induction and delivery procedures helps clinicians organize treatment options, select appropriate methods for initiating labor, and coordinate patient care within the overall framework of obstetric management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 1156
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in pregnancy is categorized under the broader group of other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother and is recognized as an obstetric abnormality of the cervix. This condition encompasses three specific subtypes: Cervical shortening, second trimester; Cervical shortening, third trimester; and Cervical shortening, unspecified trimester. Each of these subtypes is classified as early or threatened labor, indicating that a shortened cervical length at any stage of pregnancy can herald the onset of labor before term. The unspecified‑trimester subtype is also directly mapped to the concept of short cervical length in pregnancy, reinforcing its clinical definition. In addition to cervical shortening, Stenosis of cervix in pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium is another obstetric cervical abnormality, illustrating that multiple cervical pathologies can arise during gestation and the peripartum period. Together, these entities highlight the importance of assessing cervical length and integrity throughout pregnancy, as alterations such as shortening or stenosis are linked to the risk of premature or threatened labor and require careful management to optimize maternal and fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: Induction and delivery procedures encompass a range of interventions used to initiate and manage labor, with medical induction of labor identified as a specific type of procedure within this broader category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 805
retrieved_docs.content: as a child concept, integrating both modalities. Additionally, the category Other procedures inducing or assisting delivery includes the specific complication فشل التوليد بالملقط (failure of forceps delivery), highlighting a potential adverse outcome associated with assisted delivery techniques. Together, these interrelated concepts delineate a structured taxonomy of labor induction methods, their sub‑techniques, synonymous terms, and associated complications, which is essential for clinicians to navigate obstetric care decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2862
retrieved_docs.content: Early or threatened labor is classified as تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر, indicating a condition in which signs of labor appear prematurely and the situation is identified as threatened preterm labor, a designation that guides clinical monitoring and intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2857
retrieved_docs.content: The CDRH Health Effect – Clinical Code Terminology includes the concept مخاض قبل الأوان (premature labor). A poor reproductive history (REPRODUCTIVE HISTORY POOR) is clinically associated with مخاض قبل الأوان, indicating that women with suboptimal reproductive histories are at increased risk for premature labor. In addition, optional termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري) is also clinically associated with مخاض قبل الأوان, suggesting a possible link between elective pregnancy termination and the occurrence of premature labor. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in risk assessment, coding, and the management of patients susceptible to premature labor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 904
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in pregnancy is considered a component of other complications of birth and puerperium that affect the management of the mother. This condition is subdivided into cervical shortening, second trimester; cervical shortening, third trimester; and cervical shortening, unspecified trimester. Each of these sub‑categories is classified as early or threatened labor, indicating that a shortened cervix at any stage of pregnancy signals an increased risk of labor onset before term. In addition, cervical shortening, unspecified trimester is specifically mapped to the concept of short cervical length in pregnancy, reinforcing its role as a measurable indicator of potential preterm labor. Recognizing cervical shortening in its various trimester‑specific forms and its classification as early or threatened labor is essential for timely obstetric assessment and intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2028
retrieved_docs.content: Malposition, disproportion or other labor complications are classified as رَحم ناقص التوتر (uterine inertia), فشل تحريض المخاض (failure of labor induction), مرحلة أولى مطولة من المخاض (prolonged first stage of labor), and مرحلة ثانية مطولة من المخاض (prolonged second stage of labor). These classifications indicate that abnormal fetal positioning, size mismatch, or other labor difficulties can lead to inadequate uterine contractility, unsuccessful attempts to induce labor, and extended durations of both the first and second stages of labor, all of which are critical considerations for obstetric assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 204
retrieved_docs.content: or not applicable,” and a broader relationship with “previous c‑section,” situating prior cesarean delivery as a general antecedent for these findings. In the obstetric classification hierarchy, the English category “LABOR AND PARTURITION” is placed under the Arabic concept “مخاض مهدد” (threatened labor). The condition “early or threatened labor” is classified both as “تهديد بالمخاط المبتسر” (threatened premature labor) and “مخاض مهدد” (threatened labor), reflecting its status as an early or potentially premature labor scenario. Overall, these interrelated terms illustrate how a previous cesarean section and the resulting uterine scar are integrated into obstetric classification systems, influencing the categorization of labor conditions, pelvic and cervical abnormalities, and the management of maternal complications that may arise as late effects of pregnancy and childbirth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2865
retrieved_docs.content: Duration of gestation refers to the length of time a fetus remains in the uterus before birth, and the classification of a birth as liveborn is encompassed within this gestational timeframe; therefore, liveborn status is defined by the specific duration of gestation at delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2028
retrieved_docs.content: Malposition, disproportion or other labor complications are recognized as a form of failure to induce labor (فشل تحريض المخاض) and are also classified as causes of a prolonged first stage of labor (مرحلة أولى مطولة من المخاض) as well as a prolonged second stage of labor (مرحلة ثانية مطولة من المخاض). These classifications reflect that abnormal fetal positioning, size mismatch, or other intrapartum problems can impede the initiation of effective uterine contractions and can extend both early and later phases of the birthing process, highlighting the need for careful monitoring and appropriate obstetric interventions to prevent labor dystocia and improve maternal‑fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1407
retrieved_docs.content: The CDRH Health Effect – Clinical Code Terminology classification includes the concepts “قصور عنق الرحم” (cervical insufficiency) and “مخاض قبل الأوان” (preterm labor). A poor reproductive history is clinically associated with both cervical insufficiency and preterm labor, indicating that women with prior adverse reproductive outcomes are at higher risk for these obstetric complications. Additionally, the optional procedure “إنهاء الحمل - اختياري” (termination of pregnancy) is clinically associated with both cervical insufficiency and preterm labor, suggesting a link between pregnancy termination and subsequent cervical or labor abnormalities. An unknown type of cesarean section scar (“CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN”) is also clinically associated specifically with preterm labor, highlighting that uncertain scar characteristics may contribute to the risk of early labor onset. Together, these relationships underscore the importance of detailed reproductive history, careful assessment of prior pregnancy terminations, and thorough evaluation of cesarean scar status in the management and risk stratification of cervical insufficiency and preterm labor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1051
retrieved_docs.content: The domain of LABOR AND PARTURITION encompasses the obstetric condition referred to in Arabic as مخاض مهدد (threatened labor). Early or threatened labor is classified as مخاض مهدد, indicating that this specific presentation falls within the broader category of threatened labor under the umbrella of LABOR AND PARTURITION. Understanding that مخاض مهدد is a distinct subset of labor and parturition aids clinicians in recognizing early signs of labor complications and guides appropriate monitoring and management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 968
retrieved_docs.content: CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN is clinically associated with الانفصال المبكر للمشيمة (early placental separation), مخاض قبل الأوان (premature labor), and بضع الرحم (uterine atony), indicating that an unknown‑type cesarean scar may predispose to placental detachment, early onset of labor, and loss of uterine tone. Early or threatened labor has a relationship with مخاض قبل الأوان, and it is further classified as تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر (threatened preterm labor) and مخاض مهدد (threatened labor), reflecting its role as a clinical category that encompasses both general threatened labor and the specific risk of premature delivery. The broader category LABOR AND PARTURITION includes مخاض مهدد as a parent concept, situating threatened labor within the overall framework of childbirth processes. Additionally, REPRODUCTIVE HISTORY POOR is clinically associated with مخاض قبل الأوان, underscoring that a history of poor reproductive outcomes increases the likelihood of premature labor. Together, these interrelated concepts highlight how scar type, placental integrity, uterine contractility, prior reproductive history, and the classification of early or threatened labor collectively influence the risk and management of adverse obstetric events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1049
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in pregnancy is categorized under other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother and comprises three specific sub‑entities: cervical shortening, second trimester; cervical shortening, third trimester; and cervical shortening, unspecified trimester. Each of these sub‑entities is classified as early or threatened labor, which is also identified in Arabic as مخاض كاذب (false labor). The unspecified‑trimester form of cervical shortening is additionally mapped to the clinical concept of short cervical length in pregnancy. Together, these relationships illustrate that cervical shortening at any stage of gestation is regarded as a manifestation of early or threatened labor and is considered part of the broader spectrum of maternal complications that influence birth and postpartum care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1094
retrieved_docs.content: Prematurity (خديج) is closely linked to several obstetric and fetal conditions. It is clinically associated with cervical insufficiency (قصور عنق الرحم), can arise as a consequence of pulmonary hypoplasia syndrome (متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة), and is related to the occurrence of preterm labor (مخاض قبل الأوان). Cervical insufficiency itself is recognized in both the CDRH Health Effect clinical terminology and the FDA Center for Devices and Radiological Health terminology as a distinct concept. Management of cervical insufficiency often involves the cerclage procedure, and the condition is frequently observed in patients with a poor reproductive history, recurrent miscarriage (إجهاض معتاد), and those who have undergone elective termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري). Preterm labor is similarly included in the CDRH and FDA terminologies, is associated with an unknown type of caesarean section scar (CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN), and is linked to poor reproductive history and elective pregnancy termination. Additionally, the FDA terminology categorizes missed abortion (إجهاض فائت) and infertility (العقم) within its related subsets, underscoring the broader spectrum of reproductive complications that intersect with cervical insufficiency, prematurity, and preterm labor. Understanding these interconnections aids clinicians in identifying risk factors, guiding preventive strategies such as cerclage, and optimizing care for patients experiencing these intertwined obstetric challenges.
